ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Tarka Dal. Nearest parent is a cooked lentil dish or legume-based curry. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this preparation.
ontology.foodex2_description: Legume dishes — cooked yellow lentil (dal) with spice tempering (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for tarka dal)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A04CB is the closest defensible parent for cooked legume dishes. No exact code exists for tarka dal preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for tarka dal or yellow lentil curry as a prepared dish.

main_ingredients: split yellow lentils (toor dal / arhar dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of dal curry is approximately 250 g, as commonly served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Food Code G00011178)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), Hyderabad; Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (toor dal / yellow lentil curry preparation) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Food Code G00011178 for the Singapore-served preparation. Values represent a cooked tarka dal with ghee-based tempering.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — yellow split peas / pigeon peas (cooked); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 29 is referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yellow split peas / pigeon peas (toor dal), which is the primary lentil in tarka dal. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (29 × 13.5 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 9.8, rounded to 9.9 per 250 g serving. Yellow lentils consistently show GI in the 25–32 range across peer-reviewed studies.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content — 282 mg per 100 g; approximately 705 mg per standard 250 g bowl
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving provides approximately 705 mg sodium (~31% of the 2300 mg daily limit); individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, while benefiting from the dish's high potassium (285 mg/100 g) which supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 29) and high dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g) from yellow lentil base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal curry is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its GI of 29 and glycemic load of ~10 per bowl produce a minimal postprandial glucose response, and the combination of 6.2 g protein and 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g further slows glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high dietary fibre, and high folate (48 mcg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal curry supports cardiovascular health through its low saturated fat content, absence of trans fat, high soluble fibre from lentils which helps reduce LDL cholesterol, and significant folate content which supports homocysteine metabolism; sodium intake should be monitored in those with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, cumin) and high folate content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric and cumin in the tarka provide curcumin and other anti-inflammatory compounds; high folate (48 mcg/100 g) supports immune regulation, and plant protein from lentils aids muscle maintenance in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Curry (Tarka Dal) is a foundational Indian dish of slow-cooked yellow lentils — typically toor dal (split pigeon peas) or a blend of toor and moong dal — simmered until completely soft in a mildly spiced broth with turmeric, tomatoes, and onions. The defining preparation step is the 'tarka' or 'tadka': a hot tempering of ghee or oil infused with cumin seeds, minced garlic, dried red chillies, and sometimes curry leaves, which is poured sizzling over the cooked lentils just before serving, creating an aromatic, smoky flavour layer. The dish has a thick, soupy consistency with a warm, earthy, mildly spiced taste and a creamy texture from the broken-down lentils. It is served as a side dish alongside steamed rice (dal chawal), roti, thosai, or prata, and is one of the most nutritionally dense items in Singapore's Indian hawker and restaurant repertoire. Nutritionally, dal curry is high in plant protein (6.2 g/100 g), dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g), and folate (48 mcg/100 g), with a very low glycemic index of 29, making it exceptionally suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. It is naturally gluten-free, halal, and vegetarian (when ghee is used, not strictly vegan). The dish is widely consumed across India, Singapore, Malaysia, and the South Asian diaspora, and appears in Indian restaurants, mamak stalls, and home kitchens throughout Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (primary regional authority for Indian dishes) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Food Code G00011178. GI value of 29 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked yellow split peas / pigeon peas. All core macros and secondary nutrients are supported by named approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for tarka dal is not reported in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore databases as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this cooked preparation is not separately reported in the cited sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and HPB sources for this lentil-based preparation). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients contributing B12; ghee contributes negligible B12 at this serving level, confirmed per ICMR-NIN). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D-contributing ingredients present in this preparation). Portions: the input contained duplicate and conflicting portion entries; the canonical serving size of 250 g (1 bowl, verified source) is used as the default. The 300 g grabfood entry is noted as a platform-reported variant and is not used as the primary serving size per source policy. Copper (0.15 mg/100 g), manganese (0.2 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (2.5 mcg/100 g) values are from enriched_json source with confidence 0.9; these are consistent with USDA FoodData Central values for cooked lentils and are retained as supporting data.
